rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical vicinal aminoselenation and oxyselenation of alkenes (e.g., cyclohexene, styrene) using saccharin or carboxylic acids as N/O nucleophiles and diphenyl diselenide as the Se source
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of bromide (from VBImBr/Et4NBr) generates Br radical/Br2 which oxidizes PhSeSePh to PhSe radical/PhSe+; the seleniranium-type cation (alkene + PhSe+) is then opened by the N- or O-nucleophile
electron_flow: Anode oxidizes Br- to Br•/Br2, which oxidizes PhSeSePh to PhSe• and/or PhSe+; addition to the alkene gives a seleniranium (or β-selenyl carbon radical that is further oxidized to a cation), which is opened by saccharin N–H or carboxylic acid to give the vicinal aminoselenation/oxyselenation product. Cathode reduces protons to H2.

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Little effect on yield (styrene 98%; cyclohexene aminoselenation 91%; oxyselenation 92%) — minor radical involvement in early steps

probe_reagents.name: activated carbon
probe_reagents.observation: Modest decrease (83% cyclohexene aminoselenation; 75% oxyselenation) — partial radical character

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene
probe_reagents.observation: Yield drops to 87% (aminoselenation) and 84% (oxyselenation); HRMS detected vinyl selenide c' (C20H17Se, 337.0485), consistent with PhSe• addition to DPE

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Strong inhibition: styrene aminoselenation 79%, cyclohexene aminoselenation drops to 20% (0.4 mmol) and trace (0.8 mmol); oxyselenation trace. HRMS detected TEMPO–cyclohexyl–SePh adduct e', confirming a β-selenyl carbon radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: HRMS detection of seleniranium b'/d'
probe_reagents.observation: Seleniranium cations b' (from styrene, m/z 261.017) and d' (from cyclohexene, C12H15Se+ calcd 239.0334, found 239.0329) directly observed by HRMS

notes: Two mechanistic regimes are supported: (i) a radical pathway in which PhSe• adds to the alkene to give a β-selenyl carbon radical (trapped by TEMPO as adduct e'), which is oxidized to a cation and captured by N/O nucleophile; (ii) an ionic pathway via a seleniranium cation (b', d' detected by HRMS) opened by the nucleophile. The strong TEMPO inhibition and isolation of e' favor the radical-then-cation (radical polar crossover) route, while seleniranium detection indicates the cationic pathway may operate in parallel. Bromide acts as a redox mediator.
